mnemonics.title: OS BOOTSTRAP STEPS
mnemonics.content: BOOT: Basic Operations Execute Together - 1) Power On 2) Execute POST 3) Load Bootstrap from ROM 4) Initialize Hardware 5) Load OS Kernel 6) Start System Services 7) Display Login Prompt

mnemonics.title: TYPES OF INTERRUPTS
mnemonics.content: I MUST P: Interrupt types - Hardware (external), Software (programmed), Trap (error/exception), System Call (service request), Page Fault (memory)

mnemonics.title: SYSTEM CALL CATEGORIES
mnemonics.content: PFFD: Process control, File management, Device management, Information maintenance, Communication, Protection

acronyms.usage: Data structure storing process information like state, PC, registers, memory map, I/O status
System software managing hardware resources and providing services to applications
Non-volatile memory storing bootstrap program that runs on power-on

acronyms.expansion: Electrically Erasable Programmable Read Only Memory
acronyms.usage: Memory chip that can be electrically erased and reprogrammed, stores BIOS/UEFI
Data transfer between CPU and peripheral devices handled by OS
Set of system calls and functions programs use to request OS services

visualTips.visualization: Draw a timeline: POWER ON → POST → BIOS/UEFI → BOOT LOADER → OS KERNEL LOAD → SYSTEM SERVICES → LOGIN SCREEN. Each arrow represents data flow from one stage to next.

Draw two concentric circles: Inner circle = Kernel Mode (full access, all instructions), Outer circle = User Mode (restricted, limited instructions). Show transition arrows labeled 'System Call' and 'Interrupt Return'.
Draw flowchart: CPU executing → Interrupt signal → Save State → Identify Source → Call Handler → Process Interrupt → Restore State → Resume Execution. Each box shows CPU action.
Draw layers: User Application → Library (wrapper) → System Call Interface → Kernel → Hardware. Numbered arrows show request going down and response coming up.

analogies.analogy: Like a car ignition that starts the engine. Just as ignition turns on all car systems before you can drive, bootstrap initializes all computer components before the OS runs.

Like the brain and nervous system of the human body. It controls all body functions (hardware), processes thoughts (processes), and coordinates activities, while other organs (applications) perform specific tasks.
Like a bank with two areas: public lobby (user mode) where customers do limited transactions, and secure vault (kernel mode) where only authorized staff (OS) can access sensitive resources.

Like requesting a government service at a counter. You fill a form (system call), the clerk processes it (OS kernel), and you receive the service (output). You cannot directly access government files (hardware).
Like translators in international meetings. When you speak (OS gives generic command), the translator (driver) converts it to the listener's language (device-specific instruction).
Like a project manager handling multiple projects. Each project is a process with its own tasks (threads), resources (memory), and status. The manager (OS) allocates time and resources to each.
